summary: Gruber dissects a Financial Times report claiming Tim Cook is fast-tracking Apple's mixed-reality headset against the wishes of the design team to secure his legacy. He argues the FT's reporting is incomplete, omitting key players like Mike Rockwell's AR/VR division and Greg Joswiak's product marketing team, and that the design team's objection wasn't that the headset wasn't ready but that they wanted to skip it entirely and wait for AR glasses. Gruber pushes back hard on the legacy angle, arguing Cook's record is already sterling and that rushing a dud would hurt his legacy more than help it. He also reframes the supposedly modest sales projections, showing that one million units would give Apple roughly 10 percent of the global AR/VR market immediately.
key_points: The FT report omits critical voices in Apple's decision-making: Mike Rockwell's 1,000+ person AR/VR team and Greg Joswiak's product marketing division, making the narrative misleadingly binary between design and operations.
The design team's objection wasn't that the headset wasn't polished enough — they wanted Apple to skip mixed-reality goggles entirely and wait for lightweight AR glasses, which may be a decade away.
Cook's legacy is already secured through near-tenfold market cap growth, Apple Watch, AirPods, Apple Silicon, and Apple Park — he has no reason to gamble it on a rushed product.

The supposedly modest projection of one million units would actually give Apple roughly 10% of the global AR/VR headset market and potentially a majority share of worldwide revenue at $3,000 per unit.
Apple Watch deserves to be counted as a Tim Cook product, not a Steve Jobs one — it was announced three years after Jobs died.
money_quotes: Apple hasn't produced any great new products other than the great new products they've produced.
I say 'John Sculley', you hear 'Newton'. Launching a high-profile expensive dud would cause more harm to Cook's legacy than launching a successful headset would do it good.
If he thinks Apple should launch a mixed-reality headset this year, it's because he thinks doing so is in the best interest of the company, not his legacy.
The design team was never all-powerful. Jony Ive, personally, was perhaps nearly so.
Like moviemaking, product-making and platform-building are profoundly multidisciplinary endeavors, and Apple's internal culture is deeply collaborative across those disciplines.
In a certain sense, Apple under Cook is undefeated. Launch a Newton-like bust on his way out the door, however, and that might prove an indelible stain on a heretofore impeccable record.
Now that Apple Watch is undeniably a massive hit, it's a retcon to give Jobs credit for it.
key_insight: The FT's narrative of Cook gambling his legacy by overruling designers collapses under scrutiny — it omits key decision-makers, misframes the design team's actual objection, and underestimates what 'modest' sales projections would mean in the tiny AR/VR market.
